agadir_morocco.blurb: Agadir is Morocco's modern beach resort, rebuilt after a 1960 earthquake, with a long sandy bay and a hilltop kasbah ruin overlooking the port. It's also a gateway to the souks and argan-oil country of the Souss valley.
miss: The sweeping beach promenade
The hilltop Agadir Oufella kasbah ruins
The Souk El Had, one of Morocco's largest markets
agadir_morocco.tip: Agadir is more resort than old-Morocco atmosphere — for authentic souks and medinas, Taroudant inland is the better excursion if you want the real thing.

ajaccio_corsica.blurb: Ajaccio is Corsica's sunny capital and Napoleon's birthplace, with a palm-lined seafront and citadel a short walk from the pier. The island's granite mountains and coves are the draw for anyone hiring a car or taking a tour.
miss: Maison Bonaparte, Napoleon's childhood home
The morning market on Place Foch
Iles Sanguinaires sunset headland by boat or road
ajaccio_corsica.tip: The old town and market are all on foot from the ship — save any car hire for the dramatic coast road out to the Sanguinaires.

akureyri_iceland.blurb: Akureyri is Iceland's northern capital, tucked at the head of the country's longest fjord with surprising botanical gardens for its latitude. It's the gateway to the thundering Godafoss waterfall and the volcanic wonders of Lake Myvatn.
miss: Godafoss, the 'waterfall of the gods'
The geothermal fields and craters of Lake Myvatn
Whale watching from nearby Husavik
akureyri_iceland.tip: Myvatn's nature baths are a quieter, cheaper alternative to the Blue Lagoon in the south — and combine perfectly with the Godafoss and crater sights nearby.

alesund_norway.blurb: Alesund is Norway's Art Nouveau jewel, rebuilt in swirling stone after a 1904 fire and set across islands between fjord and open sea. A climb up Mount Aksla gives one of the most beautiful town panoramas in the country.
miss: The 418 steps up Mount Aksla for the view
The Art Nouveau centre and Jugendstil museum
The Atlantic Sea-Park aquarium
alesund_norway.tip: The Aksla viewpoint can also be reached by road if the steps daunt you — but the climb from the town park is direct and takes only 15-20 minutes.

alicante_spain.blurb: Alicante drops you right beside the old town on the Costa Blanca, with the Explanada palm promenade and Santa Barbara castle looming over a genuinely walkable centre. The city beach, Postiguet, is a five-minute stroll from the ship.
miss: Castillo de Santa Barbara by lift from Postiguet beach
The marble-waved Explanada de Espana promenade
Tapas in the El Barrio old quarter
alicante_spain.tip: The castle lift entrance is tucked in a tunnel across the road from the beach — far easier than the switchback climb, and cheap.

amber_cove.blurb: Amber Cove is Carnival's purpose-built gateway to the Dominican Republic's north coast, with a big pool complex and zip line inside the port itself. Outside the gates, Puerto Plata's cable car, waterfalls and rum heritage make the case for venturing out.
miss: The Teleférico cable car up Mount Isabel de Torres
The 27 Waterfalls of Damajagua canyoning trip
Puerto Plata's Victorian old town and amber museum
amber_cove.tip: The port pool is free and genuinely good — if you've done the DR before, a zero-spend pool day here is a legitimate choice.

amsterdam_netherlands.blurb: Amsterdam's cruise terminal sits right on the IJ waterfront, a walk or short tram from the canal ring, the great museums and the buzz of the centre. Few capitals put you so directly into the heart of things.
miss: A canal-ring cruise past the gabled houses
The Rijksmuseum and Van Gogh Museum
The Anne Frank House (book well ahead)
amsterdam_netherlands.tip: The Anne Frank House sells out weeks in advance with timed online tickets only — if it's on your list, book the moment your itinerary is confirmed.

antigua.blurb: Antigua claims a beach for every day of the year, and the ships dock right in St John's among the shops and rum bars. Nelson's Dockyard — the world's only continuously working Georgian dockyard — anchors the far side of the island.
miss: Nelson's Dockyard and Shirley Heights' harbour view
Valley Church or Darkwood Beach on the calm west coast
Fig Tree Drive through the rainforest interior
antigua.tip: Shirley Heights is famous for Sunday sunset parties, but the view is free and empty at midday — pair it with the Dockyard in one taxi loop.

antwerp.blurb: Antwerp is Belgium's diamond capital and Rubens's home city, with a grand Gothic cathedral and a cutting-edge fashion scene. The cruise berth is on the Scheldt right by the medieval centre, so it's a walk-off port.
miss: Rubens masterpieces in the Cathedral of Our Lady
The Grote Markt and its guildhalls
The MAS museum's rooftop city view
antwerp.tip: Antwerp station is one of the world's most beautiful — a cathedral of glass and iron. Even if you're not travelling on, it's worth the short walk to see it.

aruba.blurb: Aruba sits outside the hurricane belt with a near-guaranteed forecast: sunny, 28 degrees, windy. Oranjestad's Dutch-colonial pastels start at the pier, Eagle and Palm beaches run up the west coast, and the cactus-strewn national park fills the wild east.
miss: Eagle Beach and its famous fofoti trees
The free tram loop through Oranjestad
Arikok National Park's natural pool by jeep
aruba.tip: The constant trade wind makes the heat deceptive — you'll burn faster than you feel. The bus to Eagle Beach costs a fraction of the taxi rank.

athens_piraeus_greece.blurb: Piraeus is Athens's ancient port, a busy transport hub about 30 minutes from the Acropolis. The classical capital is the obvious draw — the Parthenon, the Plaka's tavernas and one of the world's great museums.
miss: The Acropolis and Parthenon
The Acropolis Museum's sculptures
Wandering the Plaka beneath the citadel
athens_piraeus_greece.tip: Go straight to the Acropolis on arrival before it bakes and fills — buy the combined ticket online, and enter from the quieter southeast gate if you can.

baltimore.blurb: Baltimore's cruise terminal is an industrial locale a short ride from the Inner Harbor, where the National Aquarium, historic warships and crab houses cluster around the water. Fort McHenry — birthplace of the US anthem — guards the approach.
miss: The National Aquarium's blacktip reef
Fort McHenry, of Star-Spangled Banner fame
Fell's Point's cobbled pub streets
baltimore.tip: Your sail-away passes directly under the Key Bridge site and past Fort McHenry — the harbour exit is Baltimore's best viewpoint, so linger on deck.

barbados.blurb: Bridgetown is the polished pro of southern Caribbean cruising — a UNESCO-listed capital, swimmable west-coast water, and the birthplace of rum with the distillery tours to prove it. The port is a working harbour; a shuttle covers the gate run.
miss: Swimming with turtles in Carlisle Bay
Mount Gay's rum distillery tour
Harrison's Cave in the island's limestone core
barbados.tip: Carlisle Bay's beach and turtle-swim boats are barely ten minutes from the ship — an independent taxi-and-beach day here is as easy as the Caribbean gets.

barcelona_spain.blurb: Barcelona is the Mediterranean's busiest cruise port and one of its greatest cities — Gaudi, Gothic lanes and Las Ramblas all within reach of the terminals on Montjuic. The catch is the piers are a fair way from the action, so plan the hop into town.
miss: Sagrada Familia (book a timed slot well ahead)
The Gothic Quarter and Barcelona Cathedral
Park Guell's mosaic terraces
barcelona_spain.tip: Most ships use the Adossat piers — too far to walk. Take the Blue port shuttle to the Columbus Column, then everything old is on foot from there.

bari.blurb: Bari is the capital of Puglia in Italy's heel, its atmospheric old town a warren where grandmothers make orecchiette pasta on the doorstep. It's also the gateway to the fairy-tale trulli houses of Alberobello inland.
miss: Bari Vecchia's pasta-making lanes
The Basilica di San Nicola (Santa Claus's relics)
The conical trulli houses of Alberobello
bari.tip: Wander Bari Vecchia in the morning to catch the pasta ladies at work along Strada delle Orecchiette — a genuine, unstaged bit of local life.

belfast_northern_ireland.blurb: Belfast built the Titanic, and the striking Titanic Belfast museum stands on the very slipways where she was launched. The revived city centre, the political murals and the Giant's Causeway up the coast round out a rich day.
miss: Titanic Belfast on the historic slipways
A black-cab tour of the political murals
The Giant's Causeway on the Antrim coast
belfast_northern_ireland.tip: The Giant's Causeway is a stunning but full-day coach trip. If you'd rather stay in the city, Titanic Belfast plus a cab mural tour is a powerful half-and-half.

belize_city_belize.blurb: Belize City is a long tender ride and not the destination itself — the country behind it is. This is the jumping-off point for cave tubing through jungle river caves, Mayan ruins at Altun Ha and Lamanai, and the world's second-largest barrier reef.
miss: Cave tubing the Nohoch Che'en caves
Lamanai's ruins reached by river safari
Snorkelling the barrier reef off Caye Caulker
belize_city_belize.tip: Tenders here eat 30–45 minutes each way — book something substantial and organised rather than planning to wander, because there's little to wander to.

bergen_norway.blurb: Bergen is the gateway to the fjords, its UNESCO Bryggen wharf a row of leaning wooden Hanseatic warehouses right by the harbour. A funicular climbs Mount Floyen for the classic view over the seven surrounding hills.
miss: The colourful Bryggen Hanseatic wharf
The Floibanen funicular up Mount Floyen
The bustling fish market on the quay
bergen_norway.tip: Bergen is famously rainy — the funicular is worth doing whatever the weather, as low cloud over the fjords can be as atmospheric as sunshine.

berlin_warnemunde_germany.blurb: Warnemunde is a pretty Baltic beach resort in its own right, but it's sold as the port for Berlin — which is a hefty three hours away by train or coach. The choice is a very long capital day, or a relaxed one in Rostock and on the beach.
miss: Berlin's Brandenburg Gate and Reichstag (long day)
The lighthouse and beach at Warnemunde
The Hanseatic old town of Rostock nearby
berlin_warnemunde_germany.tip: Berlin is 3 hours each way — realistically 6 hours travelling for a few hours in the city. Unless Berlin is a bucket-list must, Warnemunde and Rostock make a far gentler day.

bilbao_spain.blurb: Bilbao's ships berth at Getxo, out at the mouth of the estuary, with the Guggenheim and the revived old town a train or transfer ride upriver. The Basque city rewards the journey in — this is one of Spain's best food scenes.
miss: Frank Gehry's titanium Guggenheim Museum
Pintxos crawl through Casco Viejo
The Vizcaya transporter bridge at Getxo
bilbao_spain.tip: The Metro from Areeta (near the port) runs straight to Moyua for the Guggenheim in about 20 minutes — simpler and cheaper than a taxi across town.

bimini_bahamas.blurb: Tiny Bimini was Hemingway's fishing hideout and still feels like a sandbar with a marina attached. Ships dock at the north end; free trams shuttle to Radio Beach and the Resorts World complex with its pool and beach clubs.
miss: Radio Beach's shallow, glass-clear swimming
The Dolphin House mosaic museum in Alice Town
A conch salad shack — watch it made to order
bimini_bahamas.tip: The whole island is about seven miles long — a golf-cart rental covers all of it in an afternoon if the beach isn't enough.

bodrum_turkey.blurb: Bodrum is Turkey's stylish Aegean resort, built around a Crusader castle that houses a superb museum of underwater archaeology. It stands on the site of Halicarnassus, whose Mausoleum was one of the Seven Wonders.
miss: The Castle of St Peter and its shipwreck museum
The scant remains of the Mausoleum
The whitewashed bazaar and marina
bodrum_turkey.tip: The castle museum's Bronze Age shipwreck finds are world-class and often overlooked — allow more time inside than the modest exterior suggests.

bonaire.blurb: Bonaire is a marine park with an island attached — the entire coast is protected, and the shore diving and snorkelling are the best you can reach on foot in the Caribbean. Kralendijk is tiny, tidy and starts at the gangway.
miss: Snorkelling straight off Te Amo or Bachelor's Beach
Klein Bonaire's reef by water taxi
The pink salt flats and flamingos of the south loop
bonaire.tip: Rent snorkel gear in town and the shore entry points do the rest — this is the one port where the DIY option genuinely beats the boat tours.

bordeaux.blurb: Bordeaux is a rare treat — ships sail up the Garonne and berth right in the elegant 18th-century city centre, no shuttle needed. The world's most famous wine region sits on the doorstep, but the golden-stone city rewards staying put too.
miss: The Place de la Bourse and its water mirror
La Cite du Vin wine museum
A Medoc or Saint-Emilion vineyard tour
bordeaux.tip: Because the berth is central, you can wander the UNESCO core entirely on foot — save the excursion budget for a half-day vineyard trip if wine's your thing.

boston_massachusetts_usa.blurb: Boston's Flynn cruise terminal sits in the Seaport, ten minutes from the Freedom Trail's red brick line through 250 years of revolution — Faneuil Hall, Paul Revere's house and the USS Constitution. Compact, historic and very walkable once you're in town.
miss: The Freedom Trail from Boston Common to the North End
The USS Constitution — 'Old Ironsides' — afloat since 1797
Cannoli in the North End (join the longer queue)
boston_massachusetts_usa.tip: New England fall foliage cruises book Boston hotels solid in late September — reserve pre-cruise nights months out, not weeks.

bruges_zeebrugge_belgium.blurb: Ships berth at the industrial port of Zeebrugge, a shuttle-and-train from the impossibly picturesque medieval Bruges. With its canals, belfry and chocolate shops, Bruges is one of Europe's best-preserved old towns.
miss: Climbing the medieval Belfry for the view
A canal boat through the old town
Belgian chocolate, waffles and beer
bruges_zeebrugge_belgium.tip: Zeebrugge to Bruges takes a shuttle bus to the station then a short train — or an all-in cruise transfer. Ghent, equally lovely and less crowded, is a similar distance.

cabo_san_lucas_mexico.blurb: Cabo is the Mexican Riviera's showpiece — the granite Arch at Land's End, sea lions, and Lover's Beach are all a short water-taxi hop from where the tenders land in the marina. It's a tender port, so the marina bustle starts immediately.
miss: The Arch (El Arco) by glass-bottom boat
Lover's Beach between two seas
Medano Beach's swimmable, bar-lined sand
cabo_san_lucas_mexico.tip: Water taxis to the Arch are cheap and constant from the marina — haggle politely, agree the price and photo stops before boarding, and you don't need a tour.

cadiz_spain.blurb: Cadiz claims to be the oldest city in Western Europe, and its salt-white old town sits right at the pier — a maze of squares and sea walls you can explore entirely on foot. It's also the classic gateway to Seville and Jerez inland.
miss: The golden dome and tower of Cadiz Cathedral
Torre Tavira camera obscura over the rooftops
Playa de la Caleta between two castles
cadiz_spain.tip: Seville is doable by fast train (about 1h40 each way from the station beside the port) but it's a long day — the old town of Cadiz alone easily fills a port call.

cagliari_sardinia.blurb: Cagliari is Sardinia's capital, its honey-coloured Castello district rising in ramparts above a port you can walk straight out of. A long city beach, Poetto, stretches east, and the island's coves are a short drive away.
miss: The walled Castello quarter and its bastion terrace
Poetto beach along the eastern shore
Roman amphitheatre carved into the hillside
cagliari_sardinia.tip: The Bastione di Saint Remy terrace gives the best rooftop panorama, and it's a free climb up through the old town straight from the port.

cannes_france.blurb: Cannes is Riviera glamour distilled — the Croisette promenade, the film-festival Palais and designer windows, with the old Le Suquet quarter climbing above the harbour. Ships usually tender into the heart of it all.
miss: The Boulevard de la Croisette and its beaches
Le Suquet old town and its viewpoint
A ferry to the monastery island of Saint-Honorat
cannes_france.tip: Cannes is the classic gateway to Antibes, Grasse and even Monaco by train — the station is a short walk from the tender pier if you fancy hopping along the coast.

cartagena-spain.blurb: Cartagena's natural harbour has been a naval prize since the Carthaginians, and the ship ties up steps from a compact centre stacked with Roman ruins. The restored Roman Theatre is the star, hidden behind the old cathedral.
miss: The 1st-century Roman Theatre and its museum
Panoramic lift up Concepcion castle hill
Modernist architecture along Calle Mayor
cartagena-spain.tip: Don't confuse it with Cartagena in Colombia — this one is small and all on foot; you won't need a taxi at all.

cartagena_colombia.blurb: Cartagena's walled old town is one of the great colonial cities of the Americas — flower-draped balconies, plazas and 400-year-old ramparts, all UNESCO-listed. The port itself hides a surprise: a free aviary-and-monkey garden inside the terminal.
miss: The walled city's streets around Plaza Santo Domingo
San Felipe fortress, the largest Spanish fort in the Americas
The port terminal's own flamingos and toucans
cartagena_colombia.tip: It's seriously hot and humid by mid-morning — do the walls first, retreat to a shaded café by noon, and keep small peso notes for street vendors.

castaway_cay.blurb: Disney's Castaway Cay is the slickest private island operation afloat — the ship docks right at the pier and trams run the length of the beach. Family beaches, the adults-only Serenity Bay, and included barbecue lunch make it a zero-effort day.
miss: Serenity Bay if you're travelling without kids
Snorkelling the lagoon's sunken 'hidden Mickey' props
The 5K fun run before the beach fills up
castaway_cay.tip: Bikes and floats sell out at the rental hut — reserve online pre-cruise, and walk one tram stop further for quieter sand.

celebration_key.blurb: Celebration Key is Carnival's purpose-built Grand Bahama port, opened in 2025 and designed around five 'portals' — from the free Paradise Plaza lagoon to adults-only and family zones. It's less castaway island, more polished beach resort at scale.
miss: The huge freshwater lagoons — included, unlike much here
Starfish Lagoon's calmer family end
The suspended bridge view over Paradise Plaza
celebration_key.tip: It's big — pick your portal before you walk off and use the trams, because trudging the full length in midday heat gets old fast.

charleston_south_carolina_usa.blurb: Charleston's terminal opens onto one of America's best-preserved historic cities — antebellum mansions, Rainbow Row's pastels and the palmetto-lined Battery are all on foot. It's a homeport worth arriving early for.
miss: Rainbow Row and the Battery promenade
The City Market's sweetgrass basket weavers
A carriage tour's gossip-rich history loop
charleston_south_carolina_usa.tip: She-crab soup and shrimp-and-grits are the local orders — book dinner ahead, because Charleston's restaurant scene fills up even midweek.

cherbourg_france.blurb: Cherbourg sits at the tip of the Cotentin peninsula in Normandy, its huge harbour once the last European call for the Titanic. The Cite de la Mer maritime museum, in the old transatlantic terminal, is the standout right by the ship.
miss: Cite de la Mer and its deep-sea submarine
The Titanic-era Art Deco ferry terminal
D-Day beaches and Utah Beach museum by tour
cherbourg_france.tip: Cherbourg is a genuine gateway to the western D-Day sites — closer than from Le Havre — so it's the better call if the landing beaches are your priority.

cobh_cork_ireland.blurb: Cobh was the Titanic's final port of call and the departure point for millions of Irish emigrants, a story its harbourside heritage centre tells movingly. The colourful town, crowned by its cathedral, is walkable straight off the ship.
miss: The Titanic Experience in the old ticket office
St Colman's Cathedral above the harbour
Cork city or Blarney Castle inland
cobh_cork_ireland.tip: Blarney Castle and its famous stone are a short drive or train-and-bus away, but Cobh's own emigrant history is right at the pier and deeply worth an hour.

coco_cay.blurb: Perfect Day at CocoCay is Royal Caribbean's private island, and everything runs on your cruise card. The free side is genuinely good — Chill Island's beaches, the pool, and included lunch — while the waterpark and Coco Beach Club are paid extras.
miss: Oasis Lagoon, the Caribbean's largest freshwater pool
Chill Island's included loungers and calm swimming
The Up, Up and Away balloon view if it's flying
coco_cay.tip: Book waterpark passes before the cruise if you want them — onboard prices climb, and on sold-out days they vanish entirely.

colon_panama.blurb: Colón exists on cruise itineraries for one reason: the Panama Canal is up the road. Partial transits, the Agua Clara visitor centre overlooking the new locks, and rainforest boat safaris on Gatún Lake are the day's currency — the city itself isn't for wandering.
miss: Agua Clara locks viewing platform
Gatún Lake boat safari among monkey islands
The Panama Canal Railway's ocean-to-ocean run
colon_panama.tip: This is a book-something port — independent wandering isn't rewarding or advisable here, but the canal excursions genuinely live up to the billing.

copenhagen_denmark.blurb: Copenhagen is the coolest of the Nordic capitals — the Nyhavn canal's painted houses, the Tivoli pleasure gardens and a bike-borne, design-led way of life. Most berths are a shuttle or metro ride from the compact centre.
miss: The colourful Nyhavn harbour canal
Tivoli Gardens amusement park
The Little Mermaid statue and Amalienborg Palace
copenhagen_denmark.tip: Copenhagen's metro is driverless, cheap and fast — from the Nordhavn/Langelinie berths it beats waiting for a shuttle to reach the city sights.

corfu_greece.blurb: Corfu is the greenest of the Ionian islands, its Venetian old town a UNESCO maze of shuttered lanes and arcaded streets beneath two fortresses. The port is a short ride from the centre and the island's famous coves.
miss: The Venetian Old Fortress and Liston arcade
Achilleion palace of Empress Sisi
Paleokastritsa's turquoise bays
corfu_greece.tip: The old town is compact and shady — a good bet on a hot day — while the west-coast beaches need a bus or tour and a bit more planning.

costa_maya_mahahual_mexico.blurb: Costa Maya is a purpose-built port on a once-remote stretch of coast, with a pool-and-shops village at the pier and the fishing town of Mahahual a short ride south. Its trump card is proximity to the seldom-crowded Chacchoben Mayan ruins.
miss: Chacchoben's jungle-wrapped pyramids
Mahahual's malecón beach clubs
The Bacalar seven-colour lagoon if you have a long day
costa_maya_mahahual_mexico.tip: Mahahual's beach clubs will collect you free from the port if you commit to a lunch minimum — a cheaper, calmer day than the pier-side pool crush.

cozumel_mexico.blurb: Cozumel is the western Caribbean's heavyweight — often three or four ships at once — thanks to the reef diving that made Jacques Cousteau famous here and its ferry link to the Mayan mainland. San Miguel town is walkable from the downtown pier.
miss: Snorkelling or diving Palancar and Columbia reefs
Punta Sur eco park at the island's wild tip
El Cielo's starfish sandbar by boat
cozumel_mexico.tip: Check which pier you're on — Punta Langosta walks straight into town, while Puerta Maya and the International pier need a short fixed-rate taxi.

curacao.blurb: Willemstad's rainbow-coloured Handelskade waterfront is the Caribbean's most photographed street, and the floating Queen Emma pontoon bridge swings open for ships right beside it. Beyond town lie thirty-plus cove beaches tucked into the coast.
miss: The Queen Emma bridge swinging open
Punda and Otrobanda's UNESCO streets either side of it
Playa Kenepa's cliff-backed double cove (worth the drive)
curacao.tip: When the pontoon bridge opens for traffic, free ferries shuttle across instead — riding one is the best two-minute harbour tour in the Caribbean.

dominica.blurb: Dominica calls itself the Nature Island and means it — no big resorts, just rainforest, 365 rivers, boiling volcanic springs and the Caribbean's best whale watching. Ships dock in the middle of Roseau's charmingly weathered waterfront.
miss: Trafalgar Falls' twin cascades and hot pools
Titou Gorge's swim-through canyon
Champagne Reef, snorkelling over volcanic bubbles
dominica.tip: This is the port to spend your excursion budget on — the interior is the entire point of Dominica and it doesn't reveal itself from the pier.

dover_uk.blurb: Dover is guarded by its white chalk cliffs and a mighty medieval castle above the ferry and cruise port. It's also a gateway to Canterbury's cathedral and, for the ambitious, a long day trip to London.
miss: Dover Castle and its wartime tunnels
The White Cliffs coastal walk
Canterbury Cathedral inland
dover_uk.tip: London is possible by fast train (about an hour to St Pancras) but it's a squeeze — Canterbury is far closer and gives you a relaxed cathedral city instead.

dublin_ireland.blurb: Dublin's port is a few kilometres from the Georgian centre, an easy shuttle from Trinity College, the Guinness Storehouse and Temple Bar. Ireland's capital is compact, literary and famously convivial.
miss: The Book of Kells at Trinity College
The Guinness Storehouse gravity bar
Kilmainham Gaol's history of rebellion
dublin_ireland.tip: Book the Book of Kells and Guinness Storehouse online to skip queues — both are ticket-timed and can sell out on busy cruise days.

dubrovnik_croatia.blurb: Dubrovnik's honey-stone old town is a walled masterpiece jutting into the Adriatic — the 'Pearl of the Adriatic' and, more recently, King's Landing. Ships berth at Gruz, a short ride from the Pile Gate and the famous walls.
miss: Walking the full circuit of the city walls
The marble-paved Stradun main street
A cable car up Mount Srd for the panorama
dubrovnik_croatia.tip: Walk the walls first thing or late afternoon — midday sun on the exposed circuit is punishing, and the cruise crowds thin at the edges of the day.

edinburgh-leith.blurb: Leith is Edinburgh's historic port, now a foodie district in its own right and home to the former Royal Yacht Britannia. The castle and Royal Mile are a short bus or tram ride up into the city centre.
miss: The Royal Yacht Britannia at Ocean Terminal
Edinburgh Castle and the Royal Mile
Leith's own waterfront restaurants and bars
edinburgh-leith.tip: The tram from Leith runs straight into the city centre — a smooth, cheap alternative to a taxi, and it drops you near Princes Street for the castle.

edinburgh_south_queensferry_scotland.blurb: Ships anchor off South Queensferry beneath the three great Forth bridges, with Scotland's magnificent capital about half an hour inland. Edinburgh's castle, Royal Mile and Georgian New Town make for a superb day.
miss: Edinburgh Castle on its volcanic crag
The Royal Mile down to Holyrood Palace
The view of the Forth bridges from the water
edinburgh_south_queensferry_scotland.tip: The three Forth bridges side by side — Victorian, 1960s and modern — are a sight in themselves; grab a photo from the tender before you head for the city.

ensenada_mexico.blurb: Ensenada is Baja's port of call for short West Coast cruises — fish tacos allegedly born here, La Bufadora's blowhole thundering down the coast, and the Valle de Guadalupe wine country rising behind town.
miss: La Bufadora, one of the world's biggest blowholes
Fish tacos at the harbourside stands that invented them
A Valle de Guadalupe winery tasting
ensenada_mexico.tip: Hussong's Cantina claims the margarita's invention and looks every year of its age — one there for history, then follow the locals to the taco carts.

falmouth.blurb: Falmouth in Cornwall guards one of the world's largest natural harbours, with a maritime history the National Maritime Museum tells well. The town's independent shops and the sub-tropical gardens of the far southwest are the draw.
miss: National Maritime Museum Cornwall on the quay
Pendennis Castle guarding the harbour mouth
Sub-tropical gardens like Trebah and Glendurgan
falmouth.tip: Don't confuse it with Falmouth in Jamaica — this is Cornwall. St Ives and the Eden Project are tempting but a fair drive; the town and castle make an easy walk.

falmouth_jamaica.blurb: Falmouth's purpose-built port opens straight into one of the Caribbean's best-preserved Georgian towns. It's a base for the same excursions as Ocho Rios and Montego Bay, but the town's history walk is a genuinely good free hour.
miss: The Falmouth Heritage Walk's Georgian architecture
Glistening Waters' bioluminescent lagoon (evening calls)
Martha Brae river rafting, thirty minutes away
falmouth_jamaica.tip: The craft market inside the port gates charges port prices — walk two streets into the real town for the same items and friendlier haggling.

flam_norway.blurb: Flam sits at the head of the Aurlandsfjord, an arm of the vast Sognefjord, and exists largely for one thing: the Flam Railway, one of the world's most scenic train journeys, climbing past waterfalls to the mountain plateau.
miss: The Flam Railway mountain journey
A fjord safari by RIB boat
The Stegastein viewpoint above the fjord
flam_norway.tip: Book the Flam Railway ahead on cruise days — it sells out. Sit on the left going up for the best waterfall views, and it makes its famous photo stop at Kjosfossen.

florence_livorno_italy.blurb: Ships berth at the working port of Livorno, gateway to Florence and Pisa in Tuscany. Florence's Renaissance treasures are about 90 minutes away; Pisa's leaning tower is closer — most people pick one rather than both.
miss: Florence's Duomo and Michelangelo's David
The Uffizi Gallery (book ahead)
Pisa's Leaning Tower and Field of Miracles
florence_livorno_italy.tip: Florence and Pisa in one day is a rush. If art is the priority go straight to Florence; if you just want the photo, Pisa leaves time for a Tuscan lunch.

freeport_bahamas.blurb: Freeport is an industrial harbour, so the day is about where you go from it rather than the port itself. Most ships run shuttles to Port Lucaya Marketplace; the real prizes are the beaches and Lucayan National Park's caves.
miss: Gold Rock Beach at low tide — huge ribbed sandbars
Lucayan National Park's underwater cave system
Port Lucaya Marketplace for a low-effort browse
freeport_bahamas.tip: There's nothing to see on foot at the pier — decide between beach and park before you disembark and share a taxi to cut the fare.

funchal_madeira_portugal.blurb: Funchal clings to the green slopes of Madeira, a garden island in the Atlantic famous for its fortified wine and flowers. The seafront promenade is an easy walk; the real fun is the cable car up and the wicker toboggan down.
miss: Cable car to Monte and its tropical gardens
The Monte toboggan wicker-sled run
Madeira wine tasting in the old town
funchal_madeira_portugal.tip: Do it in the right order: cable car up to Monte first, then the toboggan run down — grown men in straw hats steer you at surprising speed.

galveston_texas_usa.blurb: Galveston is the Gulf's boom cruise port, and its Victorian Strand district — a legacy of the days it rivalled New Orleans — starts blocks from the terminals. The seawall beaches and Moody Gardens' pyramids fill out a pre-cruise day.
miss: The Strand's iron-front Victorian blocks
The 1877 tall ship Elissa at the seaport museum
Moody Gardens' rainforest pyramid
galveston_texas_usa.tip: Houston's airports are 45–90 minutes away depending on traffic — book transfers with margin, because the causeway is the only road onto the island.

gdansk_gdynia_poland.blurb: Ships berth at Gdynia, a short ride from the beautifully rebuilt Hanseatic city of Gdansk, where the Second World War began and Solidarity later ended communism. Its long market street of ornate merchant houses is a showstopper.
miss: Gdansk's Long Market and Neptune Fountain
The Museum of the Second World War
The European Solidarity Centre at the shipyard
gdansk_gdynia_poland.tip: The fast SKM train links Gdynia and Gdansk cheaply in about 35 minutes — an easy independent alternative to a coach transfer if you'd rather go solo.

geiranger_norway.blurb: Geiranger is the fjord that launches a thousand brochures — a narrow, twisting UNESCO waterway walled by cliffs and waterfalls like the Seven Sisters. The tiny village tenders you into some of the most dramatic scenery in Europe.
miss: The Seven Sisters waterfall from the water
The Dalsnibba or Flydalsjuvet viewpoints
The Norwegian Fjord Centre
geiranger_norway.tip: The sail-in and sail-out are the main event — be on deck for both. For land, the Flydalsjuvet viewpoint is a short, cheap shuttle for that iconic photo.

genoa_italy.blurb: Genoa is a proud, dense maritime republic of a city, its old town one of Europe's largest medieval quarters, tumbling down to a harbour reworked by architect Renzo Piano. Columbus was born here, and the aquarium is Italy's best.
miss: The Acquario di Genova, Italy's largest aquarium
Palazzi dei Rolli grand palaces on Via Garibaldi
The caruggi — the old town's dark medieval lanes
genoa_italy.tip: The caruggi are atmospheric but a genuine maze — pin the ship on your phone map before you dive in, as GPS struggles between the tall buildings.

gibraltar.blurb: Gibraltar is a slice of Britain on the tip of Spain, dominated by its limestone Rock and its famous apes. The town is walkable from the port; the Rock's nature reserve, caves and tunnels are reached by cable car or minibus tour.
miss: The cable car up the Rock
The Barbary macaques at the ape's den
St Michael's Cave and the Great Siege Tunnels
gibraltar.tip: Don't feed or touch the apes — they bite and steal bags. And carry your passport: nipping into Spain at La Linea means a border crossing.

glasgow-greenock.blurb: Ships berth at Greenock on the Clyde, with Glasgow's galleries, architecture and legendary music scene about 45 minutes upriver. Loch Lomond and the start of the Highlands are just as close in the other direction.
miss: Glasgow's Kelvingrove Art Gallery
Charles Rennie Mackintosh's architecture
Loch Lomond's bonnie banks
glasgow-greenock.tip: Greenock has its own train station a short walk from the berth with frequent, cheap services into central Glasgow — often quicker than an organised coach.

gothenburg.blurb: Gothenburg is Sweden's easy-going second city, built on canals and seafood, with a big amusement park and a fringe of rocky islands. It's friendlier and more relaxed than the capital, and its cafe 'fika' culture is a joy.
miss: The Liseberg amusement park
Fish market in the church-like Feskekorka
A ferry into the southern archipelago
gothenburg.tip: Take part in a proper fika — coffee and a cinnamon bun — it's a Swedish institution, and Gothenburg's cafes do it better than most.

grand_cayman.blurb: George Town is one of the Caribbean's last big tender ports, but the tenders land you straight in the walkable duty-free centre. The island's headline acts — Seven Mile Beach and the Stingray City sandbar — are both short, cheap rides away.
miss: Stingray City — waist-deep water, wild southern stingrays
Seven Mile Beach's public access at Governor's Beach
Cayman Spirits distillery tour near the pier
grand_cayman.tip: Public buses to Seven Mile run constantly from downtown for a couple of dollars — no need for the taxi rank or a ship excursion just to reach the sand.

grand_turk.blurb: Grand Turk is the rare port where the beach starts literally at the end of the pier — white sand, calm water and the cruise centre's pool all within barefoot distance. Beyond it lies a sleepy island of wild donkeys and one of the world's best walls for diving.
miss: The pier-side beach — no transport needed at all
Gibbs Cay stingray sandbar trip
Cockburn Town's weathered colonial waterfront
grand_turk.tip: You genuinely don't need an excursion here for a great day — the free beach by the ship rivals anything you'd pay to reach.

great_stirrup_cay.blurb: Norwegian's Great Stirrup Cay finally has a pier, ending decades of tender lotteries. The island is a classic Bahamas beach day — included barbecue, clear snorkelling around the rocks, and paid upgrades from cabanas to the Silver Cove lagoon area.
miss: Snorkelling around Bertha's Beach rocks
The lighthouse-end walk away from the crowds
The underwater sculpture garden just offshore
great_stirrup_cay.tip: With the new pier, port days here run longer and fuller than they used to — claim loungers early, the island fills fast now.

grenada.blurb: St George's horseshoe harbour is arguably the prettiest port town in the Caribbean, and the whole island smells faintly of its nutmeg crop. Grand Anse beach is ten minutes away; waterfalls and spice estates fill the interior.
miss: Grand Anse's two miles of calm, resort-free beach
The Underwater Sculpture Park by snorkel or boat
Fort George's harbour panorama at the top of town
grenada.tip: Water taxis shuttle from the port straight across the bay to Grand Anse for a few dollars — quicker and far more scenic than the road.

half_moon_cay.blurb: Half Moon Cay is Holland America and Carnival's shared private island, and its crescent beach is regularly rated the best in the Bahamas — two miles of powder with no buildings behind it. It's a tender port, so the day rewards an early start.
miss: The first stretch of the crescent for calm, shallow water
Horseback riding that ends with a swim in the sea
The walk to the quiet far end past the cabanas
half_moon_cay.tip: Tender queues stack up after breakfast — be on an early one and you'll have an hour of near-empty beach before the crowd lands.

halifax_nova_scotia_canada.blurb: Halifax pairs one of the world's great natural harbours with an easy waterfront boardwalk that starts at the terminal. The Citadel crowns the town, Peggy's Cove's lighthouse sits an hour away, and the Titanic story runs deep here.
miss: Peggy's Cove's granite-perched lighthouse
The Citadel's noon gun and kilted sentries
The Maritime Museum's Titanic gallery
halifax_nova_scotia_canada.tip: The Fairview Lawn Cemetery's Titanic graves are moving and rarely crowded — a short, inexpensive taxi detour most tours skip or rush.

hamburg.blurb: Hamburg is Germany's great port city, laced with more canals and bridges than Venice and crowned by the wave-roofed Elbphilharmonie concert hall. The warehouse district and the raucous Reeperbahn are both a short ride from the terminals.
miss: The Elbphilharmonie's public viewing plaza
The UNESCO Speicherstadt warehouse district
A harbour boat tour of the working port
hamburg.tip: The Elbphilharmonie's Plaza observation level is free but needs a timed ticket — grab one on arrival for a superb 360-degree view over the harbour.

helsinki_finland.blurb: Helsinki is a crisp, design-conscious Baltic capital of neoclassical squares and modernist landmarks, ringed by sea and forest. The market square, the rock-hewn Temppeliaukio church and the sea fortress of Suomenlinna are the highlights.
miss: The rock-carved Temppeliaukio Church
Senate Square and the white cathedral
The island sea-fortress of Suomenlinna
helsinki_finland.tip: Suomenlinna is a short public ferry from the market square using the same cheap city ticket — a lovely couple of hours and far better value than a tour.

heraklion_crete_greece.blurb: Heraklion is Crete's capital and the gateway to Knossos, the labyrinthine palace of the Minoans — Europe's oldest city. The Venetian harbour fort and a superb archaeological museum anchor a walkable centre.
miss: The Minoan palace of Knossos
Heraklion Archaeological Museum's frescoes
The Venetian Koules fortress on the harbour
heraklion_crete_greece.tip: See the museum before Knossos if you can — the site's best frescoes are actually in the museum, and the context makes the ruins come alive.

honolulu.blurb: Honolulu calls balance the unmissable weight of Pearl Harbor against Waikiki's surf and Diamond Head's crater rim walk. The Aloha Tower terminal sits between the two — downtown history one way, the beach the other.
miss: The USS Arizona Memorial at Pearl Harbor
Diamond Head's summit trail at first light
Waikiki Beach and a first surf lesson
honolulu.tip: Arizona Memorial tickets are released online in advance and go fast — reserve the moment your window opens rather than chancing standby.

icy_strait_point_alaska.blurb: Icy Strait Point is a cruise port owned by the Native Huna Tlingit village of Hoonah, built around a restored salmon cannery — with the world's largest zipline dropping 1,300 feet off the mountain above. The whale watching here is the best in Alaska.
miss: Whale watching in Icy Strait — humpbacks all season
The ZipRider's 90-second mountain drop
The cannery museum and Tlingit cultural performances
icy_strait_point_alaska.tip: Whales are so reliable here that operators guarantee sightings — if you're only doing one whale trip on the whole cruise, spend it at this port.

invergordon_scotland.blurb: Invergordon is the gateway to the Scottish Highlands, a working port on the Cromarty Firth. Loch Ness, Inverness and a wealth of whisky distilleries lie within reach, along with the standing stones and glens of the far north.
miss: Loch Ness and Urquhart Castle
Inverness, the Highland capital
A Speyside or Glenmorangie distillery tour
invergordon_scotland.tip: Loch Ness is the classic outing but a fair drive; whisky lovers may prefer nearby Glenmorangie or the Dalmore, practically on the doorstep at Alness.

isafjordur_iceland.blurb: Isafjordur is the capital of Iceland's remote, mountainous Westfjords, a tiny town of old timber houses hemmed in by steep cliffs. It's about wild scenery, seabird cliffs and a genuine end-of-the-world feel.
miss: The old town's 18th-century timber houses
The Dynjandi waterfall (by tour)
Arctic fox spotting and dramatic hiking trails
isafjordur_iceland.tip: This is small and weather-dependent — book any Dynjandi or hiking excursion ahead, as the town's own options are limited and fill quickly on cruise days.

istanbul_turkey.blurb: Istanbul straddles two continents, and the Galataport terminal berths you within walking distance of the old city's grand mosques, palaces and bazaars. Few ports put a city of this magnitude so directly at your feet.
miss: Hagia Sophia and the Blue Mosque
Topkapi Palace of the Ottoman sultans
The Grand Bazaar and Spice Bazaar
istanbul_turkey.tip: The main sights cluster in Sultanahmet, a tram ride or long walk from Galataport — buy an Istanbulkart for the tram, and dress modestly for the mosques.

izmir.blurb: Izmir is Turkey's relaxed, seafront third city, curving around a bay with a palm-lined promenade and a labyrinthine bazaar. It's an alternative gateway to Ephesus, but the Kordon waterfront and Kemeralti market reward staying local.
miss: The sprawling Kemeralti bazaar
The Kordon seafront promenade at dusk
The Roman Agora ruins in the centre
izmir.tip: Izmir is more laid-back than Istanbul and easy on foot — if you've already seen Ephesus, a day wandering the bazaar and Kordon is a pleasant change of pace.

juneau_alaska.blurb: Alaska's capital has no roads in — only sea and sky — and its cruise dock lands you in a walkable downtown with the Mount Roberts Tramway rising straight from the pier. Mendenhall Glacier and humpback whale watching are the twin headline acts.
miss: Mendenhall Glacier and Nugget Falls
Whale watching on Auke Bay — humpbacks near-guaranteed in season
The Mount Roberts Tramway from the dock
juneau_alaska.tip: The city 'blue bus' shuttles to Mendenhall for far less than a tour — glacier first, then spend the saving on crab legs at Tracy's shack by the dock.

ketchikan_alaska.blurb: Ketchikan calls itself the Salmon Capital of the World and greets you with Creek Street — a boardwalk of former brothels on stilts over a salmon stream. It also holds the world's largest collection of standing totem poles, minutes from town.
miss: Creek Street's stilted boardwalk (salmon run in late summer)
Totem Bight or Saxman Village's totem parks
Misty Fjords by floatplane if the budget stretches
ketchikan_alaska.tip: Rain is the local weather — locals don't use umbrellas and neither should you; a hood and quick-dry layers make the day comfortable regardless.

key_west_florida_usa.blurb: Key West is a perfect walking port — Duval Street, Hemingway's six-toed cats, the Southernmost Point buoy and Mallory Square are all within a flat mile of the pier. It's the Caribbean's atmosphere on American convenience.
miss: The Hemingway Home and its 60-odd cats
Duval Street end to end, bar by bar
Key lime pie — the frozen chocolate-dipped kind on a stick
key_west_florida_usa.tip: The Southernmost Point buoy queue is pure photo theatre — go before 10am or accept the wait. The Conch Train covers the same ground your feet can.

kiel.blurb: Kiel is a major Baltic ferry and cruise port at the end of the famous Kiel Canal, best known for its sailing regatta. The city itself is modern (rebuilt post-war); many use it as a gateway to Hamburg or Lubeck.
miss: The Kiel Canal locks at Holtenau
The maritime museum and waterfront
Lubeck's marzipan and medieval gate by train
kiel.tip: Lubeck, a UNESCO gem, is under an hour by train and arguably more rewarding than Kiel itself — the station is walkable from the berth.

kings_wharf_bermuda.blurb: Bermuda's Royal Naval Dockyard is both the cruise terminal and an attraction in its own right — a Victorian fortress complex of museums, craft markets and the National Museum. Pink-sand Horseshoe Bay and the ferry to Hamilton complete the trio.
miss: Horseshoe Bay's pink sand (bus or minibus shuttle)
The National Museum inside the Keep's ramparts
The fast ferry across the Great Sound to Hamilton
kings_wharf_bermuda.tip: Buy a transport pass at the Dockyard ferry terminal — it covers buses and ferries island-wide and pays for itself in one Hamilton round trip.

kirkwall_orkney_scotland.blurb: Kirkwall is the capital of Orkney, an archipelago richer in prehistory than almost anywhere in Europe. The red-stone St Magnus Cathedral anchors the town, while Neolithic Skara Brae and the Ring of Brodgar lie a short drive out.
miss: Skara Brae, a 5,000-year-old stone village
The Ring of Brodgar stone circle
St Magnus Cathedral in Kirkwall
kirkwall_orkney_scotland.tip: The Neolithic sites predate Stonehenge and the pyramids — book a tour ahead, as Orkney's taxis and buses are limited and cruise days fill them fast.

klaipeda_lithuania.blurb: Klaipeda is Lithuania's only seaport, a former Prussian town (once Memel) with a half-timbered old quarter. Its real treasure is across the lagoon: the Curonian Spit, a UNESCO ribbon of towering sand dunes and pine forest.
miss: The Curonian Spit's giant sand dunes
The old town's German-era architecture
The Lithuanian Sea Museum on the spit
klaipeda_lithuania.tip: The Curonian Spit needs a short ferry across the lagoon plus transport — book a tour if you want the dunes, as independent logistics eat into a port day.

kotor_montenegro.blurb: Kotor sits at the head of a fjord-like bay ringed by mountains, its walled medieval town clinging to the base of a cliff. The dramatic sail-in is half the experience; the walls zigzag up the mountain above the old town.
miss: The city walls' climb to St John's fortress
The old town's squares and Romanesque cathedral
A boat trip to Our Lady of the Rocks islet
kotor_montenegro.tip: The 1,350-step wall climb is steep and shadeless — go early with water, and the reward is a bird's-eye view straight down onto your ship in the bay.

kusadasi_ephesus_turkey.blurb: Kusadasi is the port for Ephesus, one of the best-preserved classical cities anywhere — a marble metropolis of temples, terraced houses and a spectacular library facade. The town itself is a bazaar-heavy resort; Ephesus is the reason to be here.
miss: The Library of Celsus and Great Theatre at Ephesus
The Terrace Houses' Roman mosaics
The House of the Virgin Mary nearby
kusadasi_ephesus_turkey.tip: Ephesus is 30 minutes inland and shadeless — go early, wear a hat, and enter at the top gate so you walk downhill through the ruins to the library.

la-rochelle.blurb: La Rochelle is one of Atlantic France's loveliest harbours, guarded by medieval towers and lined with arcaded streets. Ships berth at La Pallice port, a short ride from the old harbour and the bridge to the Ile de Re.
miss: The three medieval harbour towers
Arcaded old-town streets and the market
Cycling the Ile de Re's salt marshes and beaches
la-rochelle.tip: The Ile de Re is a cyclist's paradise reachable by bus over the bridge, but the compact old port alone is an easy, charming tour-free day.

la_coruna_spain.blurb: A Coruna wraps around a headland in Spain's green Galician northwest, with a glass-galleried seafront and the world's oldest working Roman lighthouse. It's also the nearest big port to Santiago de Compostela for those chasing the cathedral.
miss: The Tower of Hercules, a Roman lighthouse still lit
Glass-fronted galleria houses on Avenida de la Marina
Santiago de Compostela by coach (about an hour)
la_coruna_spain.tip: If Santiago is the goal, it's a full excursion; if you'd rather stay local, the whole peninsula walk past the tower is a lovely couple of hours on foot.

la_spezia_italy.blurb: La Spezia is the gateway to the Cinque Terre, the five candy-coloured fishing villages strung along the Ligurian cliffs. The port is walkable to the train station that links all five — this is one of Italy's great day trips.
miss: The five Cinque Terre villages by train
The clifftop walking paths between them
Portovenere's colourful harbour by boat
la_spezia_italy.tip: Buy the Cinque Terre train card at the station — hop-on-hop-off between villages all day. Go to the far village first and work back toward the ship.

labadee_haiti.blurb: Labadee is Royal Caribbean's fenced private peninsula on Haiti's north coast — a mountainous, dramatic setting unlike the flat Bahamas islands. Beaches, the huge Dragon's Breath zip line and an included barbecue fill the day.
miss: Dragon's Breath — 2,600ft of zip line over the sea
Columbus Cove's calm, shaded swimming
The artisan market for Haitian metalwork (haggle kindly)
labadee_haiti.tip: Nellie's Beach and Columbus Cove are calmer and prettier than the main Adrenaline Beach — turn right off the pier, not left.

lanzarote_arrecife_spain.blurb: Lanzarote is the Canaries' volcanic island, where artist Cesar Manrique turned lava into architecture. Ships dock at Arrecife; the island's surreal Timanfaya fire mountains and Manrique sites need a tour or hire car.
miss: Timanfaya's volcanic landscape and geyser demos
Jameos del Agua lava-tube auditorium
Cesar Manrique Foundation in a lava bubble house
lanzarote_arrecife_spain.tip: Timanfaya and the north-island Manrique sites are on opposite ends — pick one direction; trying to do both in a port day means all bus, no island.

las_palmas_gran_canaria.blurb: Las Palmas is the Canaries' biggest city, with the golden crescent of Las Canteras beach on one side and the Columbus-era Vegueta old town on the other. The port sits between them, both a short hop away.
miss: Las Canteras city beach and promenade
Casa de Colon in the Vegueta quarter
Colonial squares around Santa Ana Cathedral
las_palmas_gran_canaria.tip: Las Canteras is one of the best city beaches in Europe and walkable from the shuttle drop — bring swimwear rather than booking an excursion.

le_havre_paris_france.blurb: Le Havre is the port for Paris, though the capital is over two hours away by road. The town itself is a UNESCO-listed marvel of post-war concrete architecture by Auguste Perret — and Normandy's beaches and Honfleur are closer than Paris.
miss: Perret's reconstructed city centre and St Joseph's Church
The painterly harbour of Honfleur nearby
The D-Day landing beaches of Normandy
le_havre_paris_france.tip: Paris is a very long day (2h+ each way). Honfleur, Etretat's cliffs or the D-Day beaches give a fuller day for far less time on a coach.

limassol.blurb: Limassol is Cyprus's cosmopolitan second city, with a revamped marina, a medieval castle where Richard the Lionheart married, and the ancient sites of Kourion and Kolossi a short drive west. The port is set back, so shuttle in.
miss: Kourion's clifftop Greco-Roman theatre
Limassol Castle and the old town
The wine villages of the Troodos foothills
limassol.tip: Kourion's theatre and the ruins of ancient Amathus bracket the city — Kourion is the more spectacular if you only have time for one.

lisbon_portugal.blurb: Lisbon spills over seven hills above the Tagus, and the cruise terminal sits below the Alfama, its oldest quarter of tiled facades and rattling trams. Belem's monuments and custard tarts are a short ride west along the river.
miss: Tram 28 through Alfama's hills
Belem Tower and the Jeronimos Monastery
Original pasteis de nata at Pasteis de Belem
lisbon_portugal.tip: The 28 tram is famous and lovely but pickpocket-heavy and often mobbed — early morning straight off the ship is your best shot at a seat.

liverpool_uk.blurb: Liverpool berths you right on its UNESCO waterfront, beneath the Three Graces and steps from the Beatles story. The Albert Dock's museums and galleries make this one of the easiest big-city calls in Britain.
miss: The Beatles Story at the Albert Dock
The Royal Albert Dock museums and Tate
The Three Graces waterfront buildings
liverpool_uk.tip: The berth is central, so skip the tour — the Beatles sights, docks and cathedrals are all walkable, and the Magical Mystery bus tour is easy to grab on the day.

livorno-florence-pisa.blurb: Livorno is Tuscany's port and a likeable canal-laced city in its own right, too often skipped in the dash to Florence and Pisa. If you'd rather not spend the day on a coach, Livorno's own seafood and 'Little Venice' quarter reward staying.
miss: Livorno's Venezia Nuova canal district
A cacciucco seafood stew lunch
Florence or Pisa inland by train or tour
livorno-florence-pisa.tip: The train from Livorno reaches Pisa in about 15 minutes — a far shorter, cheaper hop than Florence if you only want the tower and a Tuscan wander.

ljubljana-koper.blurb: Ships dock at Koper, Slovenia's small Venetian-flavoured coastal town, often sold as the gateway to the capital Ljubljana or to Lake Bled. Koper's own compact old town is a pleasant walk-off; the inland gems need a transfer.
miss: Ljubljana's dragon-guarded old town and castle
Lake Bled's island church and clifftop castle
Koper's Venetian Praetorian Palace and old square
ljubljana-koper.tip: Lake Bled is the postcard everyone wants but it's a 1h30 drive each way — Ljubljana is closer and livelier; pick one inland target rather than trying both.

los-angeles.blurb: The World Cruise Center at San Pedro is LA's cruise door, with the battleship Iowa moored alongside and the rebuilt West Harbor waterfront growing next door. Hollywood and Santa Monica are a committed ride away — plan LA days around traffic, not distance.
miss: The Battleship USS Iowa beside the terminal
The Korean Bell and Point Fermin's clifftop views nearby
Santa Monica Pier if you have a full pre-cruise day
los-angeles.tip: On embarkation day, stay near the port, not in Hollywood — the 110 freeway at the wrong hour has sunk more boarding schedules than weather ever has.

lyon.blurb: A river-cruise call rather than an ocean one, Lyon is France's gastronomic capital, set where the Rhone meets the Saone. Its Renaissance old town, hidden passageways and bouchon bistros make it one of the country's most rewarding cities on foot.
miss: Vieux Lyon's traboule passageways
Fourviere basilica and Roman theatres above the city
Lunch in a traditional bouchon
lyon.tip: The traboules are covered walkways threaded through buildings between streets — a few are signposted; ducking through them is the classic Lyon experience.

malaga_spain.blurb: Malaga has shrugged off its package-holiday past to become a genuine art city — Picasso was born here — with a Moorish fortress above a palm-lined port you can walk straight into. The centre is compact, sunny and easy.
miss: The Alcazaba Moorish fortress above the centre
Picasso Museum in the old town
Rooftop cathedral and the Muelle Uno port promenade
malaga_spain.tip: Granada and the Alhambra are often sold as a Malaga excursion but it's a 90-minute drive each way — a very long day for a rushed visit. Malaga itself is underrated.

marseille_france.blurb: Marseille is France's gritty, vivid second city, built around the Vieux-Port where fishermen still sell the morning catch. The port is industrial and set apart, so shuttle in to reach the harbour, the basilica and the calanques.
miss: Notre-Dame de la Garde basilica over the city
The Vieux-Port and its fish market
Boat trip into the Calanques' limestone inlets
marseille_france.tip: Marseille is also the main gateway to Aix-en-Provence and Provence's villages — but the city itself has come a long way and rewards staying put.

mazatlan_mexico.blurb: Mazatlán's restored Centro Histórico is the Mexican Riviera's cultural sleeper — the Plazuela Machado's cafés, the Angela Peralta theatre and a 13-mile malecón patrolled by open-air pulmonía taxis. Cliff divers perform where the seawall bends.
miss: Plazuela Machado and the old town's restored streets
The cliff divers at El Clavadista
A pulmonía (open golf-cart taxi) ride along the malecón
mazatlan_mexico.tip: Ride a pulmonía at least once — agree the fare first — and time the cliff divers for when tour groups gather, as they perform when the tips assemble.

messina_sicily.blurb: Messina is Sicily's gateway across the strait from the mainland, its cathedral home to an astronomical clock that puts on a show at noon. Most visitors press on to Taormina's clifftop theatre or the slopes of Mount Etna.
miss: Taormina's Greek theatre and Etna views
Messina Cathedral's astronomical clock at noon
A 4x4 trip onto Mount Etna's lava fields
messina_sicily.tip: Time the cathedral square for noon if you stay in town — the mechanical clock's golden lion and figures are a genuine spectacle.

miami_florida_usa.blurb: PortMiami is the world's busiest cruise port, and if you're staying pre- or post-cruise the city rewards it — South Beach's Art Deco district, Little Havana's cafecito windows, and Wynwood's street-art blocks are each a short ride apart.
miss: The Art Deco district along Ocean Drive
Little Havana's Calle Ocho and a proper Cuban sandwich
Wynwood Walls' warehouse-district murals
miami_florida_usa.tip: If you're flying in for a cruise, come a day early — Miami traffic and flight delays are the classic embarkation-day heartbreakers.

monaco_monte_carlo_monaco.blurb: Monaco packs a casino, a palace and a Grand Prix circuit into two square kilometres of Riviera hillside. Ships tender into the yacht-crammed harbour, from where the old town and the famous casino square are steep but short walks.
miss: The Casino de Monte-Carlo and its square
Changing of the guard at the Prince's Palace
The Oceanographic Museum on the cliff
monaco_monte_carlo_monaco.tip: The public lifts and escalators threaded through the hillside save your legs on Monaco's slopes — locals use them constantly; look for the discreet doorways.

montego_bay_jamaica.blurb: Montego Bay is Jamaica's second city, and the cruise terminal sits in the industrial freeport — so plan on transport. Doctor's Cave Beach and the Hip Strip are ten minutes away, with rafting, great houses and luminous lagoon trips further out.
miss: Doctor's Cave Beach's famously clear water
Rose Hall Great House and its ghost story
Rafting the Martha Brae on a bamboo raft
montego_bay_jamaica.tip: Agree taxi fares before getting in — rates to Doctor's Cave are posted at the terminal, and returning drivers will find you at the beach gate at pickup time.

montreal_canada.blurb: Montréal bookends the Canada–New England run, with the Old Port's terminal steps from Old Montreal's cobbles, the Notre-Dame Basilica's blue-lit interior and a food scene that argues loudly with the rest of the continent.
miss: Notre-Dame Basilica's aurora light show
Bagels vs smoked meat — settle it at St-Viateur and Schwartz's
Mount Royal's lookout over the city
montreal_canada.tip: If your cruise starts or ends here, add a night — Montréal is a stay-and-eat city, and the Jean-Talon market alone justifies the extra day.

mykonos_greece.blurb: Mykonos is the Cyclades at their most photogenic — whitewashed cubes, blue domes and the windmills above Little Venice. It's chic, busy and pricey; the maze of Chora town is made for wandering and sunset-watching.
miss: The iconic windmills above Little Venice
Getting lost in Chora's whitewashed lanes
A boat to the sacred ruins of Delos
mykonos_greece.tip: Chora's lanes were deliberately built as a maze to confuse pirates — they'll confuse you too, which is half the charm; just keep the sea downhill to reorient.

naples_italy.blurb: Naples is chaotic, magnificent and berths you right in the centre beneath Vesuvius. Pizza was invented here, Pompeii is a short hop away, and the isles of Capri and the Amalfi Coast beckon — few ports offer so much within reach.
miss: Pompeii or Herculaneum's frozen Roman streets
Authentic Neapolitan pizza in the centro storico
Capri or the Amalfi Coast by hydrofoil or tour
naples_italy.tip: Pompeii is 30 minutes by the Circumvesuviana train from Naples — cheap and easy — but the trains are basic and crowded; go early to beat both heat and queues.

nassau_bahamas.blurb: Nassau is the Caribbean's busiest cruise port, and the rebuilt harbourfront makes it an easy self-guided day. Downtown, the Queen's Staircase and Fort Fincastle are a short walk from the ship, with Junkanoo Beach ten minutes the other way.
miss: The Queen's Staircase — 66 steps hand-carved through limestone
Junkanoo Beach, the free walk-to beach west of the port
John Watling's Distillery for a shaded rum stop
nassau_bahamas.tip: Skip the pushy taxi rank quotes — everything listed above is walkable, and Paradise Island water taxis are cheaper than cabs.

new-orleans.blurb: Cruising from New Orleans means the French Quarter is essentially part of the port — the terminal sits at the foot of the CBD, a streetcar or short walk from beignets, brass bands and the Mississippi's crescent. Come early, stay late.
miss: Café du Monde beignets in Jackson Square's shadow
Frenchmen Street for the locals' live music
A St Charles streetcar ride under the oaks
new-orleans.tip: The ship sails down 100 miles of Mississippi River after departure — sail-away here is a slow, fascinating half-day cruise in itself. Stay on deck.

new_york_new_york_usa.blurb: Sailing from New York — Manhattan's west side or Brooklyn's Red Hook — comes with the greatest sail-away in cruising: down the Hudson, past the Statue of Liberty and under the Verrazzano. The city itself needs no introduction, just extra days.
miss: The Statue of Liberty from your own deck at sail-away
The High Line to Hudson Yards near the Manhattan terminal
Times Square once, then never again
new_york_new_york_usa.tip: Book the port side of the ship (or just stand there) leaving Manhattan — Lady Liberty passes close on the left and the skyline does the rest.

nice_villefranche_france.blurb: Ships anchor off Villefranche-sur-Mer, one of the Riviera's prettiest bays, with Nice and its Promenade des Anglais a short train ride along the coast. It's the ideal base for the whole Cote d'Azur.
miss: Nice's old town and the Cours Saleya market
The pebble beach and Promenade des Anglais
Eze village and its exotic garden above the sea
nice_villefranche_france.tip: The train station in Villefranche is a walkable climb from the tender pier and links Monaco, Nice and Antibes cheaply — the coast is yours for a few euros.

nuuk_greenland.blurb: Nuuk is the world's smallest capital with a skyline — colourful blocks against raw mountains at the mouth of a vast fjord system. The National Museum's Qilakitsoq mummies and the old colonial harbour anchor a compact, strollable visit.
miss: The Greenland National Museum's 500-year-old mummies
The old Colonial Harbour and Hans Egede's church
A boat trip into the Nuuk fjord if time allows
nuuk_greenland.tip: Weather rules everything in Greenland — have a flexible plan and treat any sunny hour as the moment to do the outdoor thing immediately.

ocean_cay_msc.blurb: Ocean Cay is MSC's converted industrial sand-mining island, now a marine reserve — and it shows in the water clarity. The ship stays docked late into the evening on most calls, with the lighthouse show after dark as the signature moment.
miss: The lighthouse light show after sunset
North Beach for the calmest, clearest swimming
The evening on-island time most other private islands don't offer
ocean_cay_msc.tip: This is the rare private island where ships stay until late — plan a shore dinner-and-lightshow evening rather than cramming it all in by 4pm.

ocho_rios_jamaica.blurb: Ocho Rios is Jamaica's original cruise town, with Dunn's River Falls — the island's most famous excursion — just five minutes from the pier. The town itself is walkable, but the day really belongs to the falls, gardens and river adventures around it.
miss: Climbing Dunn's River Falls (water shoes essential)
Mystic Mountain's rainforest bobsled ride
Konoko Falls for a quieter falls alternative
ocho_rios_jamaica.tip: Dunn's River is heaving when the ships are in — book the earliest slot you can or flip the day and go after 1pm when the tour groups thin out.

olbia_sardinia.blurb: Olbia is the gateway to Sardinia's glittering Costa Smeralda, the playground of super-yachts and celebrities. The town itself is modest; the appeal lies in the emerald bays and the resort villages a short drive north.
miss: The beaches of the Costa Smeralda
Porto Cervo's yacht-set harbour
Olbia's Romanesque basilica of San Simplicio
olbia_sardinia.tip: The famous beaches need a car or tour — Olbia town is a quick wander. If you want emerald water, book transport ahead as taxis are scarce and pricey.

olden_norway.blurb: Olden lies at the head of the Nordfjord, the jumping-off point for the Briksdal and Kjenndal glacier arms of the mighty Jostedalsbreen ice cap — mainland Europe's largest glacier. It's raw, green, waterfall-laced Norway.
miss: Briksdal Glacier and its meltwater lake
The Loen Skylift cable car to Mount Hoven
Lake Lovatnet's turquoise waters
olden_norway.tip: The Loen Skylift whisks you 1,000m up in minutes for a jaw-dropping fjord panorama — a great weather-independent alternative if the glacier hike is off.

oslo_norway.blurb: Oslo berths you right below the Akershus fortress in the heart of the Norwegian capital, an easy walk to the opera house you can climb and the Munch museum. The Viking and polar-exploration museums lie across the fjord.
miss: Walking on the roof of the Oslo Opera House
The new Munch museum and National Museum
The Fram polar ship and Viking Ship museums
oslo_norway.tip: The Bygdoy peninsula museums (Fram, Kon-Tiki, Viking ships) are reached by a fun little ferry from the pier — a nicer approach than the bus.

palermo_sicily.blurb: Palermo is Sicily's boisterous capital, a heady mix of Arab-Norman architecture, baroque churches and some of Europe's best street-food markets. The port drops you within walking distance of the chaotic, compelling centre.
miss: The golden mosaics of the Cappella Palatina
Ballaro or Vucciria street-food markets
Monreale Cathedral's mosaics just outside town
palermo_sicily.tip: Do the street food properly — arancini, panelle, sfincione — the markets are the real Palermo, and far more memorable than a sit-down lunch.

palma_de_mallorca_spain.blurb: Palma's vast sandstone cathedral, La Seu, dominates the bay and the ship's approach. Beyond it lies a surprisingly elegant old town of courtyards and boutiques — the island capital is far more refined than Mallorca's resort reputation suggests.
miss: La Seu cathedral, with a Gaudi-reworked interior
The Arab Baths and old-town patios
Bellver castle's circular ramparts above the city
palma_de_mallorca_spain.tip: The vintage wooden train to Soller through the mountains is a wonderful half-day, but it leaves from Palma centre — factor in the shuttle time before you commit.

panama-city.blurb: Panama City calls (usually at Fuerte Amador) pair the canal's Miraflores locks with a skyline the rest of Central America can't match, plus the colonial Casco Viejo quarter. It's often a partial-transit bookend, so check your ship's timings.
miss: Miraflores locks visitor centre
Casco Viejo's plazas and rooftop bars
The Amador Causeway's skyline view
panama-city.tip: Traffic in Panama City is genuinely bad — build slack into any independent plan and treat all-aboard time with more respect than usual.

port_canaveral_florida_usa.blurb: Port Canaveral is the cruise gateway to Orlando's parks — but its own neighbour is the better story: Kennedy Space Center is twenty minutes away, and rocket launches are now frequent enough that one might share your sail-away.
miss: Kennedy Space Center and the Atlantis shuttle
Cocoa Beach and its famous pier
A launch, if the schedule gods smile
port_canaveral_florida_usa.tip: Check the launch schedule for your embarkation or return date — watching a rocket climb out over the Atlantic from a ship's deck is a genuine bucket-list freebie.

port_everglades_fort_lauderdale_florida_.blurb: Port Everglades in Fort Lauderdale is one of cruising's big three Florida homeports, fifteen minutes from its own airport. The city's canals earn the 'Venice of America' tag, and the beach promenade is a fine pre-cruise wind-down.
miss: A water taxi loop through the canal mansions
Las Olas Boulevard's restaurant row
Fort Lauderdale Beach's wave-wall promenade
port_everglades_fort_lauderdale_florida_.tip: FLL airport is so close you can be ship-to-terminal in 15 minutes — but morning-of flights before 11am are still gambling with disembarkation queues.

portland-maine.blurb: Portland lands you straight into the Old Port's cobbled lanes of fish shacks, breweries and boutiques — the best small-city walk-off on the New England circuit. Lighthouses, lobster boats and Casco Bay islands fill anything longer.
miss: Portland Head Light, Maine's postcard lighthouse
A lobster roll — decide your butter-vs-mayo allegiance
The Old Port's boutiques and micro-breweries
portland-maine.tip: Portland Head Light is a short taxi away in Cape Elizabeth and beats every in-town view — go there first, eat lobster after.

porto.blurb: Porto gave port wine its name, and the city tumbles in tiled tiers down to the Douro, its riverside lodges still ageing barrels across the water in Gaia. Ships berth at Leixoes, a short ride from the atmospheric old centre.
miss: Port lodge tastings in Vila Nova de Gaia
Livraria Lello, the ornate 'Harry Potter' bookshop
The azulejo-tiled Sao Bento station hall
porto.tip: Leixoes to the old town is 20-30 minutes — the ship shuttle or a taxi is worth it; the Metro trip involves a change and a walk.

porto_leixoes_portugal.blurb: Leixoes is Porto's deep-water port, in the seaside suburb of Matosinhos north of the city — known locally for the best grilled seafood in the region. The historic Porto riverfront and port lodges are a short transfer inland.
miss: Grilled sardines and seafood in Matosinhos
Porto's Ribeira riverside (by transfer)
The striking wave-form cruise terminal itself
porto_leixoes_portugal.tip: If you love seafood, don't rush straight to the city — the Matosinhos grills right by the terminal are what Porto locals travel out for.

portsmouth.blurb: Portsmouth is the Royal Navy's historic home, its dockyard packed with legendary warships from Nelson's HMS Victory to the Tudor Mary Rose. The Spinnaker Tower and waterfront are an easy walk from the terminal.
miss: HMS Victory and the Historic Dockyard
The Mary Rose Museum
The Spinnaker Tower's glass sky-walk
portsmouth.tip: The Historic Dockyard ticket covers multiple ships and museums and easily fills a day — buy the all-attraction pass rather than paying per ship.

princess_cays.blurb: Princess Cays sits on the southern tip of Eleuthera, a straightforward beach-and-barbecue day split into two halves — the busier main beach by the tender pier and a quieter stretch beyond the water-sports hut. Everything charges to your cruise card.
miss: The quieter beach south of the pier
Snorkelling the rocky points at either end
The included beach barbecue — go early or late
princess_cays.tip: Shade is the scarce resource here, not sand — grab a spot under the trees or book a clamshell as soon as you land.

puerto_vallarta_mexico.blurb: Puerto Vallarta pairs a golden-sand bay with a genuinely charming old town — the Malecón's sculptures, the crowned church of Our Lady of Guadalupe and the Rio Cuale island market. The Zona Romántica's cobbles are the town at its best.
miss: The Malecón boardwalk's sculpture parade
The Zona Romántica and Playa Los Muertos pier
A panga boat to Yelapa or the southern coves
puerto_vallarta_mexico.tip: Local buses marked 'Centro' run from outside the port for pennies — the old town is fifteen minutes and a world away from the terminal malls.

puntarenas_costa_rica.blurb: Puntarenas is Costa Rica's Pacific cruise door, a long sandspit town that serves as launchpad to the country's real riches — cloud forests, zip lines, volcanoes and wildlife-thick rivers. Pura vida starts about thirty minutes inland.
miss: Ziplining or hanging bridges in the rainforest canopy
A Tárcoles River crocodile safari
Sarchí's painted oxcarts and coffee country
puntarenas_costa_rica.tip: Distances deceive here — Monteverde and Arenal are too far for a port day, so aim for Tárcoles, Carara National Park or canopy tours within an hour.

quebec_city_canada.blurb: Québec City is the closest thing North America has to a walled European town, and ships dock directly beneath it. The funicular links the Petit-Champlain's stone lanes to the Château Frontenac's boardwalk above; Montmorency Falls waits just outside town.
miss: Terrasse Dufferin's boardwalk beside the Château
Petit-Champlain — the continent's oldest shopping street
Montmorency Falls, higher than Niagara
quebec_city_canada.tip: The funicular queue is worth skipping if you can manage the Breakneck Stairs — and October calls come with peak fall colour on the Laurentians.

reykjavik_iceland.blurb: Reykjavik is the world's northernmost capital, a small, colourful city of corrugated houses beneath the soaring Hallgrimskirkja church. It's the launchpad for Iceland's Golden Circle geysers, waterfalls and the Blue Lagoon.
miss: The Golden Circle: Geysir, Gullfoss and Thingvellir
The Blue Lagoon geothermal spa
Hallgrimskirkja church tower over the city
reykjavik_iceland.tip: The Blue Lagoon needs pre-booking and is nearer the airport than the city — check it fits your port hours, or the closer Sky Lagoon is an easier soak.

rhodes_greece.blurb: Rhodes has one of Europe's finest medieval walled cities, built by the Knights of St John and still lived-in behind its moat and gates. The port sits right beside it, so the Palace of the Grand Masters is a short walk away.
miss: The medieval Old Town and Street of the Knights
The Palace of the Grand Master
The Acropolis of Lindos down the coast
rhodes_greece.tip: The Old Town is walkable from the ship, but Lindos with its clifftop acropolis is the island's other great sight — pick one, as together they're a rush.

riga_latvia.blurb: Riga is the largest of the Baltic capitals, famous for a spectacular concentration of Art Nouveau architecture alongside a cobbled medieval core. The port is close to the old town, all easily explored on foot.
miss: The Art Nouveau district on Alberta iela
The House of the Blackheads on Town Hall Square
The bustling Central Market in old Zeppelin hangars
riga_latvia.tip: Look up as you walk Alberta and Elizabetes streets — Riga has more Art Nouveau facades than anywhere in Europe, and the detail is in the upper floors.

rio-de-janeiro.blurb: Rio needs no sales pitch — Christ the Redeemer, Sugarloaf's cable car and Copacabana's mosaic promenade deliver exactly what the postcards promise. The Pier Mauá terminal sits by the revitalised port district and its striking Museum of Tomorrow.
miss: Christ the Redeemer by cog train
Sugarloaf's two-stage cable car at golden hour
Copacabana and Ipanema's beach promenade
rio-de-janeiro.tip: Do Corcovado early — clouds swallow the statue by many afternoons — and use official taxis or apps rather than street hails, keeping valuables minimal.

roatan_honduras.blurb: Roatán sits on the Mesoamerican reef and delivers the best-value snorkelling and diving in the western Caribbean. Ships dock at Mahogany Bay or Coxen Hole; West Bay's white-sand beach with reef swimming straight off it is the classic day.
miss: West Bay Beach — reef snorkelling from the sand
Little French Key's island day resort
The sloth and monkey sanctuaries (with expectations set)
roatan_honduras.tip: Mahogany Bay's chairlift to its private beach is fine, but West Bay is the beach the island is famous for — share a taxi and agree the return time.

rome_civitavecchia_italy.blurb: Civitavecchia is the port for Rome, though the Eternal City is over an hour inland by train or coach. It's a full, foot-pounding day to see the Colosseum, Vatican and fountains — pace it, or explore the coast instead.
miss: The Colosseum and Roman Forum
St Peter's Basilica and the Vatican Museums
The Pantheon and Trevi Fountain
rome_civitavecchia_italy.tip: Pre-book Vatican and Colosseum entry before you sail — the on-the-day queues can swallow hours you don't have on a port call.

rotterdam.blurb: Rotterdam is the Netherlands' bold architectural showcase, rebuilt after wartime devastation into a city of cube houses, daring bridges and Europe's biggest port. It's also a springboard for Delft, The Hague and Kinderdijk's windmills.
miss: The Cube Houses and Markthal food hall
The Erasmus Bridge and modern skyline
Kinderdijk's UNESCO windmills nearby
rotterdam.tip: Amsterdam is doable by train (about an hour) but Rotterdam's own architecture is unique in Europe — and Kinderdijk's windmills are a more restful, iconic day.

saint-john-nb.blurb: Saint John, New Brunswick sits on the Bay of Fundy, home of the world's highest tides — the Reversing Falls rapids literally flow backwards twice a day. The Victorian uptown and the covered City Market start right at the terminal.
miss: Reversing Falls Rapids at the right tide time
The 1876 City Market's dulse and maple stalls
Hopewell Rocks' flowerpot formations on longer calls
saint-john-nb.tip: Check the tide table first — the Reversing Falls only 'perform' around the turn of the tide, and cruise mornings don't always line up.

san-diego.blurb: San Diego's terminal sits on the downtown Embarcadero beside the USS Midway's flight deck — the Gaslamp Quarter, Little Italy and ferry to Coronado are all walkable. It's the easiest big-city homeport on the West Coast.
miss: The USS Midway museum next to the pier
Coronado's beach and grand old hotel by ferry
Balboa Park's museums and gardens
san-diego.tip: The Midway is literally beside the cruise terminal — even a two-hour pre-boarding visit works if you check bags at the port first.
