san-francisco.blurb: San Francisco's ships berth along the Embarcadero near Fisherman's Wharf, with the sea lions of Pier 39, cable cars climbing from the turnaround and Alcatraz out in the bay. Sailing under the Golden Gate is the harbour's mic-drop.
miss: Alcatraz — book weeks ahead, it sells out
A cable car over the hills to Union Square
The Ferry Building's food hall
san-francisco.tip: Sail-away under the Golden Gate Bridge is the single best deck moment in US cruising — it's usually cold and windy up there, so take a layer, not just a phone.

san-juan.blurb: Old San Juan is one of cruising's great walk-off ports — five hundred years of blue cobblestones, pastel facades and two vast Spanish fortresses, all starting a few steps from the gangway. No transport, no plan needed; just walk uphill and get lost.
miss: El Morro fortress and its kite-flying lawn
Calle Fortaleza's colour and the old city gates
A barrachina piña colada at its claimed birthplace
san-juan.tip: Do the forts early — the walls give no shade and the trade wind on El Morro's lawn is the town's best free air conditioning.

santa_cruz_de_tenerife_spain.blurb: Santa Cruz is Tenerife's capital, with Calatrava's wave-like concert hall by the water and Spain's tallest peak, Teide, brooding inland. The centre is walkable; the volcano and old town of La Laguna need transport.
miss: Auditorio de Tenerife's sail-like concert hall
Mount Teide National Park by cable car (via tour)
UNESCO old town of San Cristobal de La Laguna
santa_cruz_de_tenerife_spain.tip: Teide is spectacular but a long haul up — if you're prone to altitude queasiness the cable car top station is over 3,500m, so take it gently.

santorini_greece.blurb: Santorini is the drowned volcano everyone pictures when they think of Greece — white villages perched on a caldera cliff above a wine-dark sea. Ships tender to the base of the cliff; a cable car or donkey path climbs to Fira.
miss: The caldera-edge village of Oia
Fira's clifftop cafes and sunset views
The Minoan excavations at Akrotiri
santorini_greece.tip: The cable car queue after tendering can be brutal both ways — go up early, and start queuing to come down well before your last tender to avoid missing it.

santos-sao-paulo.blurb: Santos is São Paulo's seaside and the busiest port in Latin America — but for visitors it means beach gardens that hold a world record, a coffee-boom heritage and Pelé's own museum in the club where he played.
miss: The beachfront gardens — the world's longest
The Coffee Exchange's stained-glass trading hall
The Pelé Museum near Santos FC's ground
santos-sao-paulo.tip: São Paulo city is 80km and heavy traffic away — unless you have a late departure, Santos itself is the better-value day.

savona_italy.blurb: Savona is Costa Cruises' Ligurian home port, a workmanlike town with a handsome old core and the Priamar fortress by the sea. It's also a springboard for Genoa and the Riviera villages a short train ride along the coast.
miss: The Priamar fortress on the waterfront
The old town and Sistine Chapel of Savona
Genoa or the Riviera by train
savona_italy.tip: Savona's own centre is a pleasant walk, but if you've seen it before, the coastal train to Genoa or Noli is quick and scenic.

seattle.blurb: Seattle is the Alaska cruise gateway, and both terminals sit within reach of the essentials — Pike Place Market's fish throwers, the original Starbucks queue you can skip, and the Space Needle. Add a day for the full coffee-and-mountains effect.
miss: Pike Place Market early, before the crowds
The Chihuly Garden and Glass beside the Space Needle
The Great Wheel and waterfront oyster bars
seattle.tip: Pier 91 (most big ships) is a ride from downtown while Pier 66 is walkable — check which one you're on before planning embarkation morning.

seville-cadiz.blurb: Some itineraries list this call as Seville, but ships berth at Cadiz on the coast — Seville itself is inland, reached by coach or fast train. The Andalusian capital rewards the trip with its cathedral, Alcazar and flamenco soul.
miss: Seville Cathedral and the Giralda tower
The Real Alcazar's Mudejar palaces and gardens
Plaza de Espana's tiled grandeur
seville-cadiz.tip: It's roughly 1h40 each way — a genuine full-day commitment. If time is tight, Cadiz's own old town at the pier is a rewarding alternative.

seward_alaska.blurb: Seward sits at the head of Resurrection Bay with Kenai Fjords National Park on its doorstep — the tidewater-glacier and whale-watching boat trips from here are among Alaska's best. The Alaska SeaLife Center anchors the compact town.
miss: A Kenai Fjords cruise to Aialik or Holgate glacier
The Alaska SeaLife Center's rescued puffins and sea lions
Exit Glacier's walkable viewing trails
seward_alaska.tip: Turnaround days here are tight for the big boat tours — if Kenai Fjords is the dream, consider staying a night rather than squeezing it into embarkation day.

sitka_alaska.blurb: Sitka was Russian America's capital, and it remains the most atmospheric town on the Alaska circuit — onion-domed St Michael's Cathedral, totem-lined rainforest trails and a raptor rescue centre, all against a volcano-backed sound.
miss: Sitka National Historical Park's totem trail
The Alaska Raptor Center's rehabilitating eagles
St Michael's Russian Orthodox cathedral
sitka_alaska.tip: Most ships dock at Old Sitka Dock with free shuttles the five miles to town — factor the shuttle queue into your all-aboard arithmetic.

skagway_alaska.blurb: Skagway is the gold rush preserved — false-front streets run straight from the dock, and the White Pass & Yukon Route railway climbs 3,000 feet through the mountains the stampeders crossed on foot. Book the train; it's the point of the port.
miss: The White Pass & Yukon Route railroad
The Klondike Gold Rush historical park's free walking tour
The Red Onion Saloon's brothel-museum cheek
skagway_alaska.tip: White Pass trains sell out weeks ahead in summer — book before the cruise, and sit on the left going up for the drop-away canyon views.

southampton_uk.blurb: Southampton is Britain's great cruise home port, from which the Titanic sailed and much of the UK fleet still departs. As a call it's usually an embarkation day, but the SeaCity Museum and medieval walls make a decent stretch of the legs.
miss: SeaCity Museum's Titanic story
The medieval town walls and Bargate
Winchester or the New Forest nearby
southampton_uk.tip: As a turnaround port it's mostly logistics, but if you have hours to spare, Winchester's cathedral is a 20-minute train ride and a lovely half-day.

split_croatia.blurb: Split is built inside a Roman emperor's retirement palace — Diocletian's 1,700-year-old walls now shelter a living warren of bars, shops and homes. The ferry port drops you steps from the palace and the palm-lined Riva promenade.
miss: Diocletian's Palace and its cellars
The cathedral in the emperor's mausoleum
The Riva waterfront and Marjan hill park
split_croatia.tip: The palace isn't a museum you enter — it's the old town itself, free to wander. The atmospheric substructure cellars are the one paid bit worth doing.

st_kitts.blurb: St Kitts packs a lot into a small island — a UNESCO fortress on Brimstone Hill, the scenic railway built for the sugar crop, and green vervet monkeys that outnumber people. Port Zante lands you straight into compact, walkable Basseterre.
miss: Brimstone Hill Fortress, the 'Gibraltar of the West Indies'
The St Kitts Scenic Railway's coastal loop
Timothy Hill's two-ocean viewpoint
st_kitts.tip: Timothy Hill's photo stop looks over Atlantic and Caribbean at once — every island taxi tour includes it, so a shared cab beats a formal excursion on price.

st_lucia.blurb: St Lucia is the drama queen of the eastern Caribbean — the twin Piton peaks, a drive-in volcano and rainforest all crowd the south while ships dock in workaday Castries. It's a big island; the best of it needs a committed excursion day.
miss: The Pitons from Soufrière — by boat beats by road
Sulphur Springs' drive-in volcano and mud baths
Marigot Bay for a shorter, prettier half-day
st_lucia.tip: Soufrière is 90 winding minutes each way by road — take a catamaran down the coast instead and you get the Pitons from the water plus the drive back skipped.

st_maarten.blurb: Philipsburg gives you two countries in one port day — the Dutch side's beaches and shopping, and the French side's food and style over the open border. The famous jet-blast beach at Maho sits under the airport approach twenty minutes away.
miss: Maho Beach's landing jets, close enough to touch
The water taxi across Great Bay to Front Street
Lunch in Grand Case on the French side
st_maarten.tip: Check the arrivals board before committing to Maho — the show is only as good as the flight schedule, and the big jets cluster around midday.

st_thomas_usvi.blurb: Charlotte Amalie combines duty-free shopping (US visitors get a raised allowance here) with one of the Caribbean's prettiest harbours. Magens Bay's postcard beach and the Skyride viewpoint are the classic half-days; St John's national park beaches are the upgrade.
miss: Magens Bay — the beach on all the posters
The Skyride to Paradise Point above the harbour
A ferry side-trip to Trunk Bay on St John
st_thomas_usvi.tip: Open-air safari taxis run fixed per-person rates to Magens Bay — cheaper than tours, and the 99 Steps in town fill the gap if you split the day.

stavanger_norway.blurb: Stavanger pairs a charmingly preserved old town of white timber cottages with Norway's oil-boom prosperity. It's the base for Lysefjord and the vertiginous Pulpit Rock, though that's a serious hike rather than a stroll.
miss: Gamle Stavanger's white wooden lanes
A Lysefjord cruise to Pulpit Rock's base
The Norwegian Petroleum Museum
stavanger_norway.tip: Hiking to the top of Pulpit Rock is a demanding 4-5 hour round trip — most cruisers take the fjord sightseeing boat that views it dramatically from below instead.

stockholm_sweden.blurb: Stockholm spreads across fourteen islands where lake meets Baltic, its medieval Gamla Stan old town among the best preserved in Europe. The approach through the archipelago is a highlight in itself.
miss: Gamla Stan's medieval lanes and royal palace
The Vasa Museum's salvaged 17th-century warship
The ABBA Museum for a singalong
stockholm_sweden.tip: The Vasa — a warship that sank on her maiden voyage and was raised almost intact — is Scandinavia's must-see museum; go early before the coach parties land.

stornoway_scotland.blurb: Stornoway is the main town of the Isle of Lewis in the Outer Hebrides, a Gaelic-speaking outpost of peat, tweed and ancient stones. The Callanish standing stones and pristine beaches are the island's treasures.
miss: The Callanish standing stones
Lews Castle and its museum
Harris Tweed weavers and Hebridean beaches
stornoway_scotland.tip: Callanish is often called Scotland's Stonehenge and is far less crowded — the drive across the peat moor is bleakly beautiful; book transport in advance.

tallinn_estonia.blurb: Tallinn has one of Europe's best-preserved medieval old towns — a fairy-tale of spires, cobbles and merchant houses inside intact city walls. The port is a walkable stroll from the gates, making it one of the Baltic's easiest calls.
miss: The medieval Town Hall Square
Toompea hill and the Orthodox cathedral
Walking the old town walls and towers
tallinn_estonia.tip: The old town is genuinely walkable from the ship — save your money for a marzipan treat or a craft-beer stop rather than a tour you don't need.

tampa_florida_usa.blurb: Tampa's cruise terminals sit right in the downtown Channel District, an easy walk or streetcar ride from the aquarium, Sparkman Wharf and the Riverwalk. Historic Ybor City — the cigar quarter with free-roaming chickens — is a streetcar away.
miss: Ybor City's cigar-era streets by free streetcar
The Florida Aquarium beside the terminals
The Riverwalk to Armature Works food hall
tampa_florida_usa.tip: The TECO streetcar from the port to Ybor City is free — Tampa is the rare homeport where a car-free pre-cruise day works perfectly.

tortola_bvi.blurb: Road Town is the low-key capital of the British Virgin Islands, and the port is really a springboard — to Cane Garden Bay over the mountain, to the Baths' giant boulders on Virgin Gorda, or just to a beach bar with a painkiller cocktail.
miss: The Baths on Virgin Gorda — worth the ferry
Cane Garden Bay's beach-bar crescent
The mountain-road viewpoints en route to either
tortola_bvi.tip: If the Baths are the goal, book a tour or early ferry immediately on landing — independent ferry slots on ship days are the island's scarcest commodity.

toulon_france.blurb: Toulon is France's great naval harbour, less polished than its Riviera neighbours but all the more genuine, with a Provencal old town and a cable car up Mont Faron. It's a quieter, cheaper gateway to Provence.
miss: Cable car up Mont Faron for the harbour panorama
The Provencal old town and morning market
A harbour boat tour past the naval fleet
toulon_france.tip: Toulon is a handy alternative gateway to Aix and the Provence villages, but the local old town and Mont Faron make a relaxed, tour-free day.

trieste.blurb: Trieste is Italy's Habsburg outlier near the Slovenian border, a grand coffee-house city that once served as Vienna's seaport. Its vast seafront square, Piazza Unita, opens right onto the Adriatic beside the ship.
miss: Piazza Unita d'Italia, Europe's largest sea-facing square
Miramare Castle on its promontory
The historic Viennese-style coffee houses
trieste.tip: Trieste takes coffee seriously and has its own vocabulary for it — ask for a 'nero' for an espresso; 'un caffe' may not get you what you expect.

valencia_spain.blurb: Valencia pairs a medieval core with Calatrava's futuristic City of Arts and Sciences, and it's the birthplace of paella. The port is industrial and set back, so you'll shuttle or ride in to reach either the old town or the science city.
miss: City of Arts and Sciences complex and aquarium
The Central Market and Silk Exchange
Authentic paella in the old riverbed gardens
valencia_spain.tip: The old town and the City of Arts are at opposite ends of the drained-river park — the 25 bus links them, or hire a bike along the greenway between.

valletta_malta.blurb: Valletta is a fortress-city built by the Knights of St John, its golden bastions rising straight from the Grand Harbour where the ship berths. Compact and packed with baroque splendour, it's one of the Mediterranean's great walled capitals.
miss: St John's Co-Cathedral and its Caravaggio
The Upper Barrakka Gardens over the harbour
The Grand Master's Palace and armoury
valletta_malta.tip: The Barrakka Lift carries you from the waterfront up into the city for a small fare — far kinder than the steep climb, and the garden views at the top are free.

vancouver.blurb: Vancouver's Canada Place terminal is the prettiest embarkation in North America — downtown on one side, seaplanes and mountains on the other, and Stanley Park's seawall starting a short stroll away. Alaska sailings begin with the Lions Gate Bridge overhead.
miss: Stanley Park's seawall by bike or foot
Granville Island's public market by mini-ferry
Gastown's steam clock and brunch scene
vancouver.tip: Sail-away under the Lions Gate Bridge is the trip's first highlight — be on deck, because the ship clears it by what looks like inches.

venice_italy.blurb: Venice needs no introduction — a city of canals, palazzi and bridges with no equal on earth. Large ships now berth away from the historic centre and shuttle you in, but the sail toward the lagoon and the walk into St Mark's remain unforgettable.
miss: St Mark's Basilica and Square
The Rialto Bridge and Grand Canal by vaporetto
The Doge's Palace and Bridge of Sighs
venice_italy.tip: Skip the pricey gondola if budget matters — the number 1 vaporetto water bus travels the whole Grand Canal for a few euros and is the great Venetian ride.

verona.blurb: Usually reached as an excursion from the Venetian ports, Verona is Romeo and Juliet's city, built around a remarkably intact Roman arena that still hosts summer opera. Its rose-marble streets and riverside setting are pure romance.
miss: The Roman Arena, still used for opera
Juliet's balcony on Via Cappello
Piazza delle Erbe's frescoed market square
verona.tip: Juliet's balcony is a tourist scrum with no real history — the Arena and the piazzas are the true rewards; don't let the balcony crowds define the day.

victoria-bc.blurb: Victoria is the classic evening call on Alaska round-trips — a few hours of British-flavoured charm around the Inner Harbour, the illuminated Parliament, and the Empress Hotel's ivy. Butchart Gardens is the excursion if your hours allow.
miss: The Inner Harbour and Parliament lights after dusk
Butchart Gardens (check timing against your call)
Fisherman's Wharf's float homes and fish and chips
victoria-bc.tip: Most calls here are short and in the evening — decide in advance between Butchart's gardens and the harbour stroll, because doing both is a squeeze.

vienna.blurb: A Danube river-cruise highlight, Vienna is the imperial Habsburg capital of palaces, coffee houses and Klimt. Its ring of grand boulevards, the Hofburg and Schonbrunn palaces, and its musical heritage fill a day effortlessly.
miss: Schonbrunn Palace and its gardens
St Stephen's Cathedral in the old centre
Klimt's 'The Kiss' at the Belvedere
vienna.tip: Vienna's coffee houses are UNESCO-listed cultural institutions — lingering over a Melange and a slice of Sachertorte is a legitimate sightseeing activity here.

vigo_spain.blurb: Vigo is Galicia's fishing powerhouse, tumbling down a hillside to a working port where the oyster stalls of Rua da Pescaderia sell straight off the boats. It's also a launch point for the pristine Cies Islands in season.
miss: Fresh oysters on Rua das Ostras
The old town Casco Vello lanes
Cies Islands ferry (summer, weather permitting)
vigo_spain.tip: The Cies Islands are glorious but the ferry needs a permit and decent weather — have the oyster-and-old-town plan ready as the reliable fallback.

whittier.blurb: Whittier is a working port in a spectacular dead-end fjord, reached through a one-lane shared rail-road tunnel — and almost everyone who lives here shares one building. It exists on itineraries as the rail gateway to Anchorage, Denali and the interior.
miss: The 26 Glacier Cruise from the small-boat harbour
Portage Glacier en route to Anchorage
Begich Towers — the town that lives in one building
whittier.tip: If you're ending your cruise here, the scenic rail to Anchorage beats the coach — same tunnel, better windows, and glaciers to the door.

zadar.blurb: Zadar is a quieter Dalmatian gem, a Roman and Venetian old town on a peninsula famous for two modern marvels: a Sea Organ that plays with the waves and a solar 'Greeting to the Sun'. The port is a short walk from it all.
miss: The wave-powered Sea Organ at sunset
The circular St Donatus church and Roman forum
The Greeting to the Sun light installation
zadar.tip: Alfred Hitchcock called Zadar's sunset the world's most beautiful — time your walk to the Sea Organ for dusk and you'll see why.
